text: Laparoscopic diaphragmatic hernia repair with mesh. New Hanover Regional Medical Center, Department of Surgery. Wilmington, North Carolina. A forty-nine year old female presents with right flank pain for several days. She first noted the pain after sexual intercourse. She denies vomiting but endorses nausea. She denies changes in bowel movements. No chest pain or shortness of breath. She denies previous surgery or history of trauma. Preoperative imaging revealed large right posterior diaphragmatic hernia with colon in the thoracic cavity.

The patient is positioned in the lateral decubitus position with the left side down. The surgical exploration reveals a hernia defect in the posterior aspect of the right diaphragm. A large amount of omentum and colon is visible in the right chest cavity, which is reduced, exposing a seven by five centimeter hernia defect. The right chest cavity is fully visualized, with no peritoneal hernia sac present.

The surgeon mobilizes the right lobe of the liver and exposes the left lower aspect of the diaphragmatic defect through retroperitoneal dissection, reflecting the right kidney medially. The diaphragmatic defect is closed primarily using figure-of-eight permanent sutures, starting medially and progressing laterally towards the chest wall with the endostitch device.

The surgeon uses a suture passer to close the diaphragmatic defect laterally due to increased tension on the primary repair. A coated propylene mesh is placed over the entire defect and tacked into place. The right kidney and liver are repositioned to their normal anatomical locations, covering most of the mesh.

The patient did well postoperatively. She was discharged home on postoperative day one in stable condition. She returned to the hospital one month later with cold cystitis. At that time, a CT scan was obtained. Here you can see that there is no residual hernia defect. The preoperative CAT scan is on the left. The postoperative CAT scan is on the right.
